data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Celsius Surges On Alani Nu Deal
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Beverage jolt: Celsius (CELH) skyrockets after inking deal to acquire Alani Nu. (00:24) Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY) to seek investment from Tesla (TSLA) after fallout with Honda (HMC) - report. (01:34) YouTube (GOOGL) (GOOG) plans to roll out 'premium lite' in major markets - report. (02:46)

This is an abridged transcript.

Celsius Holdings (NASDAQ:CELH) skyrocketed in late trading on Thursday after the company announced an acquisition of Alani Nutrition LLC for $1.8 billion.

Alani Nu was founded in 2018 and is a female-focused brand that offers functional beverages and wellness products targeting Gen Z and millennial consumers.

John Fieldly, Chairman and CEO of Celsius, expressed enthusiasm for the acquisition, highlighting the respect for Alani Nu's strong community of supporters and authentic brand partnerships.

Financially, the acquisition is expected to enhance Celsius' (NASDAQ:CELH) topline growth and also be cash EPS accretive in the first year of ownership. Notably, the company anticipates achieving $50 million in run-rate cost synergies over two years post-close.

The energy drink deal has implications for PepsiCo (PEP), as a holder of a large stake in Celsius (CELH), as well as rivals Coca-Cola (KO), Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP), Zevia (ZVIA), and Monster Beverage (MNST), all competing for beverage market share.

Celsius (CELH) is up 35% in premarket action.

A high-level Japanese group, including former Prime Minister Yoshihide Suga, is proposing that Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) invest in Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY) following the fallout of merger talks with Honda (HMC).

According to a Financial Times report that cites people with direct knowledge of the matter, this initiative, led by ex-Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) board member Hiro Mizuno, aims to position Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) as a strategic investor, particularly interested in acquiring Nissan's (OTCPK:NSANY) manufacturing plants in the U.S.

Merger talks with Honda (HMC) were terminated last week, which has likely intensified Nissan's (OTCPK:NSANY) search for strategic alliances to bolster operations in the face of ongoing financial challenges.

Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY) stock jumped ~10% on the Japanese stock exchange after the news.

Separately, Moody's has downgraded Nissan's (OTCPK:NSANY) rating to junk, saying the decision "reflects Nissan's weak profitability driven by slowing demand for its aging model portfolio".

"Even if the company successfully executes its restructuring plan with cost reductions and new model releases, we do not expect free cash flow to turn positive until fiscal 2026 at the earliest," Moody's added.

YouTube (GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) is planning to introduce a new, lower-priced version of its paid video service.

Bloomberg reported Thursday that the service will target viewers who primarily want to watch programs other than music videos.

The “premium lite” YouTube plan will be announced soon in the U.S., Australia, Germany, and Thailand, the report said, citing a person familiar with the plans.

The Verge previously reported that the premium lite plan was being tested in overseas markets for months. The plan is now expected to officially roll out more broadly, including in the U.S. for the first time.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

New No. 1: Amazon takes the quarterly revenue crown from Walmart

Walgreens Boots drops amid report Sycamore bidding for Family Dollar chain

Lilly's next obesity drug serves up dramatic weight loss -- and side effects: report

Catalyst watch:

  • Sandisk (SDK) will start trading after being spun off from Western Digital (WDC). The business split will create two independent, publicly traded companies focused on different aspects of data storage technology.

  • The CAGNY Conference will include presentations from Celsius Holdings (CELH) and Nestle (OTCPK:NSRGY).

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is down 0.8% at $71/barrel. Bitcoin is up 1.2% at $98,000.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is up 0.1% and the DAX is flat.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Block (NYSE:XYZ) shares tumbled nearly 7% after missing Q4 earnings estimates and issuing a disappointing outlook for Q1 and FY2025.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 10:00 am Existing Home Sales

  • 10:00 am Quarterly Services Survey

  • 11:30 am San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly will give opening remarks before the Macroeconomics and Monetary Policy Conference hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco.

  • 11:30 am Federal Reserve Vice Chair Philip Jefferson will speak on "Central Bank Communication" before the Macroeconomics and Monetary Policy Conference hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Garmin Q4 2024: Record Revenue Masks Underlying Risks
data.description:
After releasing headline-grabbing Q4 2024 Earnings results, I think it's a great time to check back into how things are going with the wearable technology company, Garmin Ltd. (NYSE:GRMN). The last time I took a bullish stance on the company was back in November 2023, because of its cost and time-efficient vertically integrated business model and its investment into new product releases and diversification. Since then, the stock price has increased by 108.56% and the top and bottom-line results have shown upward trending results for three consecutive financial years.

In Q4 2024 we saw record revenue, an EPS increase of 40% and the company generously increasing its dividend by 20%. The recent headlines and historic performance look good. If the stock were cheaper, I would stick to my bullish recommendation. But at its current premium valuation, the potential impact of competition, its loss-making Auto OEM segment and the rising geopolitical risks in Taiwan are not worth the risk at this price point. Therefore, as a cautious investor, I would recommend a wait-and-see hold rating.

Q4 2024 Recap

Garmin has without a doubt released some impressive results to finish off its financial year with a 20% YoY increase in sales to hit $1.82 billion. This is a company that is not just relying on its existing product line, it is investing in product innovation across a diverse range of segments, frequently releasing new products in the last year. Just a quick Google and your search results will be filled with product releases across a wide range of segments. This diversification has been a key driver of revenue growth. We can see double-digit growth across a number of sectors, including Fitness in which revenue grew by 31% YoY, Outdoor which grew by 29% thanks to a high demand for adventure watches and Auto OEM which saw revenue growth of 30% YoY.

Unfortunately, Auto OEM becomes an eyesore when looking at operating income. Despite its revenue growth, the segment had an operating loss of $39 million due to a 10% increase in operating expenses and higher-than-expected foreign currency losses. Conversely, the loss is only a small percentage of the total income and as a result, there is little to fault in its FY 2024 performance.

However, due to the high valuation, I wanted to take a more critical look into the potential cracks.

Potential company concerns

If we look further than just the financial results, three potential issues stand out when analysing Garmin's future performance. The first concern is namely competition within its Fitness segment from big-name competitors in the form of Apple (AAPL) and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF). While Garmin's Fitness segment income growth grew by 108% the highly competitive market is showing signs of saturation as seen below in the graph. The fight for market share could put pressure on pricing and negatively impact margins.

The second concern lies within its Auto AEM segment, which has shown persistent losses YoY in FY2024. Management indicated softened demand from major automakers. A few years, back, it was believed that this segment would bring in $800 million in 2025. This will be far from the reality with a guidance of 7% revenue growth for FY2025, and the question arises whether a potential divestiture or maybe even a partnership could improve the value here.

Lastly, the company has seen soft demand within its Marine segment. We could see a 17% YoY revenue growth, but only 6% was due to organic growth, most of the growth came from the JL Audio acquisition. Although it is great to see the boost that this acquisition has given to total revenue, investors should still be wary about the potential integration costs.

Valuation

Garmin is a company that has proven itself a sturdy long-term stock to hold, with returns of 362.82% over the last decade. Like many companies on the stock market, it took a Covid-19 hit, but we can now see that the stock is trading at historical highs. If we look at the valuation, we can see that the stock valuation is much higher than its five-year average across all metrics, not including the FWD PEG non-GAAP of 1.86. A lot of this valuation is based on the strong momentum and growth the company experienced in FY2024. However, from the company's guidance and concerns previously addressed, it appears unlikely that the company can repeat the same set of results in FY2025.

Garmin's guidance shows an 8% revenue growth and an EPS of $7.80 for FY2025, which aligns with analyst consensus. However, there are definitely some risks to consider for the upcoming year.

Risks

A risk that investors may want to be wary of is that a very large part of the company's manufacturing takes place in Taiwan. As it stands right now, we are still not sure to what extent the rising tensions between the US and China tariff relations could potentially disrupt and seriously hurt the company's financial performance. Another issue is that there appear to be growing issues with software updates and neglect of its legacy products. Software glitches and issues with the technology are well documented on the internet and could negatively impact its financial performance if these are neglected or issues continue. Lastly, the company increased its dividend by 20%, while pleasing investors, it could hurt the company in the long run if they neglect to invest this money into R&D for innovations, necessary to stay relevant in a competitive market.

Final thoughts

On a financial front, the company has delivered a strong performance, and it is great to see three financial years of top and bottom-line growth. On top of that, the company has rewarded investors with a dividend hike of 20%. However, the stock price has increased incredibly over the last year and the premium pricing warrants more caution. With a more critical eye, tough competition, loss-making segments and softening demand in FY2025 make the valuation harder to chew. Therefore, for now, I recommend a hold rating until we have better clarity as to a potential Auto OEM turnaround and the demand at play in 2025.

data.title: DHS: Not Many Benefits Besides Better Resilience During A Market Downturn
data.description:
Overview

WisdomTree U.S. High Dividend Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:DHS) operates as a diversified fund that aims to provide investors with exposure to dividend companies that have higher than average dividend yields. The fund has a very reasonable net expense ratio of 0.38% and differentiates itself from other traditional dividend focused ETFs by paying out its distributions on a monthly basis. However, the current dividend yield sits around 3.5%, which isn't really that much higher than peer dividend ETFs that share a similar goal. While my personal dividend focused portfolio puts more of an emphasis on individual holdings, I am always on the hunt for a solid ETF that can be used as an equalizing force to create balance across all holdings.

The idea that a dividend ETF will be able to provide a sufficient amount of capital appreciation and dividend growth over long periods of time and on a consistent basis. In order for DHS to be a worthy ETF, it must compete with some of the more popular dividend funds such as Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), so I will be providing some comparison data here as well. DHS has a public inception dating back to 2006, so we have almost two decades worth of performance data to reference. Looking over the last ten years, we can see that the price has appreciated by over 56%. When including all distributions paid during this time frame, the total return jumps up to 126%.

Although DHS has provided positive returns, the ETF underperforms peers for a few crucial reasons that I will discuss. Additionally, the fund's sector allocation lacks some meaningful exposure to areas that offer a lot more potential for capital appreciation. While DHS technically gets the job done, I don't see any compelling reasons to initiate a position here. So let's start by taking a look at the fund's portfolio allocation and investment strategies and covering the strengths and weaknesses.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's fact sheet, net assets currently sit at $1.14B and is spread across 381 different individual holdings. Looking at the sector breakdown, we can see that exposure to Financials account for the largest portion of assets at 19.82%. This is followed by weight in Health Care and Consumer Staples, making up 19.67% and 18.63% net assets respectively. Although DHS is technically diversified, the combination of these three sectors make up over 58% of net assets. Therefore, we can assume that the long-term growth of these sectors will have the greatest influence over DHS's success and daily price movement.

Looking at the top ten holdings, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) is currently the largest holding with a weight slightly over 5%. This is followed by a 4.97% weight in Exxon Mobil (XOM) and a 4.47% weight in AbbVie (ABBV). I generally like the mix of holdings here because DHS mixes together the companies with long-established histories of dividend raises with the companies that also have a higher than average starting dividend yield.

I'll give you two examples of what I am referring to with its holdings make up. Chevron (CVX) has a higher than average starting dividend yield of about 4.4%. CVX has been able to effectively raise their dividend payout every single year for 37 years straight. Another example would be Altria Group (MO), which provides the best of both worlds by delivering a higher starting yield and large dividend growth. MO has raised dividend payouts for over 55 consecutive years, earning the company the title of a dividend king. The company provides a high starting dividend yield of 7.7%, while also being able to increase the dividend at an average annual rate of 7.18% over the last decade.

Strategy Weakness & Strength

While DHS provides exposure to companies that have historically paid dividends out to shareholders over time, the issue with this allocation is that it lacks a meaningful amount of exposure to companies that are capable of providing a higher amount of capital appreciation. This is a common weakness that I see with dividend focused ETFs and it makes sense since technology companies aren't known for their consistent dividend payments. Technology companies are more likely to reinvest their earnings back into the business, which is why we see valuations appreciate much more rapidly.

The emphasis on dividends means that DHS is exposed to the slower moving sectors such as consumer discretionary, utilities, real estate, and materials. To demonstrate the effect of this lower technology allocation, we can see a price growth comparison between DHS and its sibling fund WisdomTree U.S. Quality Dividend Growth Fund (DGRW). The key distinction between the two funds would be the word growth. In a recent article I published titled, 'Beat The S&P 500 With An ETF Portfolio That Grows Dividend Income', I stated that I would have DGRW as my largest position in an ETF-only portfolio. This is because DGRW selects holdings based on metrics like dividend coverage strength, free cash flow, ability to increase dividends, and a meaningful tech allocation that makes up nearly a fourth of its assets. We can see how DGRW severely outperforms DHS with a slight tweak in its strategy.

However, the ETF is constructed in a way that is more defensive in nature. Dividend paying companies is sometimes the place where investor capital flows during bearish environments. The consistent dividend income they provide can offset market slowdowns. In order to see a demonstration of this, we can look back at the performance of DHS for the entire calendar year of 2022 when markets were declining as a result of extremely high inflation and a shifting interest rate environment.

Looking at the comparison below, we can see that DHS's price remained positive throughout 2022, delivering a modest growth of 4.2%. However, the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) fell by about 19.5% during the same time frame. So in downward markets, DHS's focus on dividend paying companies can act as a defensive hedge that protects against general market volatility. There's a psychological benefit of receiving a dividend payment when markets are declining and it makes it a lot easier for investors to remain invested.

Goldman Sachs issued a forecast that estimated the S&P 500's average annual return to only amount to 3% over the next decade. The basis of the report is that tech companies such as the 'Mag 7', have driven most of the positive market growth over the last decade. Analysts believe that this continued high rate of earnings and sales growth is unsustainable, based on historical data. For instance, only 11% of S&P 500 companies since 1980 have been able to consistently grow sales by over 10% for at 10 consecutive years.

I don't personally expect there to be a slowdown when we consider the different tech related catalysts that exist right now. Things like AI, growing demand for data centers, more efficient software, and other tech focused innovations can all be a driving force of growth in the markets. However, I wanted to mention this Goldman estimate to provide a market environment that may favor a fund like DHS. If capital were to flow out of the tech sector, it could make its way to these dividend focused sectors.

Dividend Comparison

The latest declared monthly dividend amounts to $0.12 per share, bringing the current dividend yield to 3.5%. Looking at the dividend history over the last decade, the payouts have continually increased and rewarded shareholders with a larger annual income year after year. For instance, DHS has been able to increase its dividend at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 6.95% over the last ten years. On a smaller time frame of five years, DHS has been able to increase its dividend at a CAGR of about 4%.

Although the dividend growth may be sufficient, it drastically underperforms peer dividend ETFs. To be fair, DHS doesn't explicitly state that dividend growth is one of its goals. However, I think that dividend growth is an important metric that many income-seeking investors care about. It can be a powerful wealth building tool to have a growing stream of supplemental income that outpaces inflation. While the terms 'high yield' are in the name of the fund, a starting yield of 3.5% can barely be considered high. After all, the dividend yield is about the same as Schwab's U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

SCHD is comprised of companies that have a proven history of growing dividends and have the potential to continue increasing dividend payouts in the future. SCHD is arguably one of the most popular ETFs due to its consistent performance and double-digit dividend growth rate. If we compare the two funds against one another, SCHD is the clear winner over the last the decade because of the higher total return. Additionally, SCHD's price has more than doubled over the last decade.

SCHD's dividend growth significantly outpaces the growth that DHS has been able to deliver. For instance, SCHD has been able to increase its dividend payout at a CAGR of 11.04% over the last decade. Even on a smaller time frame of 5 years, SCHD has been able to increase the dividend at a double-digit CAGR of 11.59%. To demonstrate the difference that a larger dividend growth rate makes, I ran a back test of an initial $25,000 into each of these ETFs at the start of 2016.

The blue bars represent the income received from DHS and the green bars represent the income received from SCHD. The graph assumes that no additional capital was ever deployed into these positions after the initial $25,000 investment. However, the calculation does assume that all dividends received were reinvested back into their respective funds. Here were the results:

2016 Dividend Income

  • DHS: $940
  • SCHD: $825

2024 Dividend Income

  • DHS: $1,978 - represents a 110% increase in income.
  • SCHD: $2,541 - represents a 208% increase in income.

Therefore, DHS underperforms SCHD as investors would be able to experience a better total return and a better income growth rate with SCHD. It's pretty difficult to make a case for DHS since it has demonstrated underperformance against peers in both dividend income and price appreciation.

Takeaway

In conclusion, DHS gets the job done by providing investors with instant diversification across some of the most well-known dividend paying companies in the world. The ETF is diverse across sectors but lacks a meaningful technology allocation that would help capture a higher level of price appreciation during bull markets. Additionally, the fund is described as a 'high dividend' yielding ETF, but the yield is on par with peers like SCHD. SCHD also significantly outpaces DHS in terms of dividend growth over time. The only benefit that I see with DHS is that it may be less volatile than traditional indexes during a market decline. So if you are seeking a better sense of stability and less volatility, DHS may be for you. Other than this, I do not see a compelling reason to own this underperforming ETF.

data.title: XMMO Is Poised To Outperform S&P 500 In 2025
data.description:
The mid-cap category is poised to generate healthy returns in 2025 amid economic growth, rate cuts and improved earnings. Their recent financial performance and 2025 guidance also demonstrate a strong support for the share price acceleration and dividend returns. Furthermore, mid-caps are undervalued with strong earnings growth power. Investing in mid-caps for the long term could also be a great strategy given their high-growth potential and low valuations compared to large caps. Furthermore, a mid-cap momentum strategy can help investors beat the mid-cap 400 and large-cap S&P 500 indices. Therefore, I initiate coverage of Invesco S&P MidCap Momentum ETF (NYSEARCA:XMMO) with a buy rating

Mid-Caps Poised to Outperform the S&P 500 in 2025

As mid-caps have a higher sensitivity to economic and monetary conditions compared to large-caps, it appears that rate cuts and economic growth are likely to create a positive impact on their business performance. According to the IMF data, the US GDP is likely to grow around 2.7% in 2025 and accelerate to more than 3% in the following two years. In addition, the Fed is expected to cut rates around 3.50% to 3.75% by the end of the year, down significantly from the peak of 5.50% to 5.75%. Consequently, favorable conditions are also likely to fuel mid-caps earnings growth power. According to the Vanguard data, the S&P MidCap 400 index is expected to deliver 13% earnings growth in 2025.

Furthermore, their sluggish recent share price performance combined with double-digit earnings growth outlook make mid-caps significantly undervalued compared to the large-caps. The S&P MidCap 400 index currently trades around 20x trailing and 18x forward earnings compared to the S&P 500’s 28x and 22x, respectively. Other characteristics that make mid-caps an interesting play include their significant growth potential because they are in a growth stage with a room for rapid expansion. Their business models are also well-established compared to small companies to cope with the tail-events and market cycles.

Historical trends also suggest that mid-caps are poised to deliver robust returns in 2025 and ahead. For instance, data shows that a mid-cap category outperformed the broader market index in easing monetary conditions, particularly twelve and twenty-four months after the rate cuts. As the Fed began cutting rates in September 2024, it's highly likely that low credit costs and improved economic conditions can help mid-caps accelerate their uptrend in the coming months and quarters. In addition, data highlights that mid-caps can produce lofty returns when the economy is growing above 2%. Overall, it appears that mid-caps are well-positioned to offer healthy returns to investors in 2025 because of favorable market conditions.

How Is XMMO Set to Outperform the S&P MidCap 400 and S&P 500 in 2025?

Although mid-caps underperformed in the past years, investment options like Invesco S&P MidCap Momentum ETF can help investors outperform the traditional mid and large cap indices in the short and long run. XMMO tracks performance of the S&P MidCap 400 Momentum Index. The fund’s share price rallied 25% in the last twelve months, topping the S&P 500 gain of 22%. A mid-cap momentum strategy also outperformed the S&P 500 in the past three, five and ten years.

XMMO's outperformance is attributed to its portfolio management. The fund is composed of 78 stocks with the top 10 representing 28% of the overall portfolio. In addition to lowering the concentration in top holdings, XMMO’s diversification factor is high with investments spreading across all sectors. The fund rebalances its portfolio semi-annually to align its portfolio with market trends. Furthermore, the robust financial and share price performance of XMMO's top holdings vindicate optimism in the growth prospects of mid-caps in 2025 and beyond. For instance, the share price of Williams-Sonoma (WSM), XMMO's largest stock holding, rallied 90% in the past twelve months as improved market fundamentals began reflecting in its financial results. In the latest reported quarter, the company topped estimates and boosted its guidance.

Sprouts Farmers Market (SFM), XMMO’s second-largest holding, experienced a blistering 233% share price growth in the last twelve months, thanks to its double-digit revenue and earnings growth. The company expects 12% year-over-year revenue growth in 2024 while its adjusted diluted earnings per share is expected in the range of $3.64 to $3.68 compared to $2.50 per share in the previous year. EMCOR Group (EME), a construction and engineering company, continued to set new records across its key financial and operational metrics. Wall Street expects a 57% year-over-year increase in its 2024 earnings per share. The company’s share price rallied 72% in the past twelve months. Whereas, Interactive Brokers Group (IBKR) generated a 125% increase in its share price in the past twelve months as the automated electronic broker continued to impress investors with a sustainable growth trend across its business. In 2024, Interactive Brokers customer accounts increased 30% to 3.34 million while customer equity and daily average revenue trades increased 33% and 61%, respectively.

Besides the Carlisle Companies Incorporated (CSL), the rest of its top holdings also generated exceptional share price returns in the past twelve months. For example, Equitable Holdings (EQH) shares surged 60% in the last twelve months, while the share price of Curtiss-Wright Corporation (CW), Tenet Healthcare Corporation (THC), Evercore (EVR) soared 43%, 49% and 47%, respectively. Overall, the mid-caps' robust financial performance and share price momentum indicate that they are benefiting from economic growth and easing monetary conditions.

The fund’s characteristics, such as a low price to earnings ratio compared to the S&P MidCap 400 index and the S&P 500, make it an attractive investment option. XMMO’s share are currently trading around 19x trailing and 16.80x forward earnings. Its price to book ratio is also lower than other indices. Its low valuations are attributed to its holdings' robust earnings growth power. Furthermore, its expense ratio of 0.39% is lower than the median of all ETFs. XMMO’s liquidity factor is also strong given $3.98 billion in assets under management and 604K in average daily share trading volume. The ETF earned a buy rating based on Seeking Alpha quant analysis.

Risk Factors to Consider

Although broader economic trends appear favorable, there are multiple risk factors to consider when using aggressive investment strategies. XMMO's performance can be impacted by the lower than expected rate cuts in 2025. In addition, a slower than expected economic growth could also negatively impact its performance. The fund’s standard deviation of 23 is high from the median of all ETFs while beta of 1.13 signals a greater downside risk during downtrends.

In Conclusion

A mid-cap category appears well-positioned to generate healthy returns in 2025 because of favorable market conditions, strong earnings growth power and cheap valuations. In fact, investors can beat the mid-cap and large-cap indices through investment options like Invesco S&P MidCap Momentum ETF. The ETF’s exposure to fast-growing mid-caps increases its share price growth potential while cheap valuations and strong earnings growth power create room for a long-lasting uptrend. XMMO’s low expense ratio, healthy liquidity and diversification factor also make it a solid investment option for the long term.

data.title: AppLovin Stock: Let It Drop Further Before Stepping In
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

I first wrote about AppLovin Corp. (NASDAQ:APP) in mid-October 2024 with a "Hold" rating, which I then confirmed in early January 2025, assuming that 2025 should be different from 2024 with APP's valuation looking unjustified amid their future EPS growth projections appearing overly optimistic. The recently released Q4 FY2024 results from a few days ago provided another boost to the stock price - since my last update APP is up over 28%:

However, as I can see from yesterday's price action, the stock dropped by about 9% while the NASDAQ 100 (NDX) closed -0.42% lower. The main thing driving the dip seems to be the recent research report by a short-seller, with allegations that the "firm's revenue growth is of "low quality".

It is becoming a common practice among short-sellers to issue reports when a high-growth stock increases 5-10 times in value over a short period with limited downsides along the way (the APP stock is up 725% on a YoY basis). However, let us focus on conventional data - the latest AppLovin's earnings report and third-party sources with market forecasts - to assess the upside potential remaining for prospective AppLovin investors today.

AppLovin Q4 2024 Earnings Review

The firm closed the Q4 FY2024 quarter with consolidated sales of $1.37 billion (+44% YoY) and an adjusted EPS of $1.73 (+253% YoY), crushing the consensus forecasts by 8.73% and 37.53%, respectively (Seeking Alpha data):

The management called Q4 FY2024 "arguably their most foundational period since the AXON upgrade in 2023" as they made a successful expansion beyond their traditional gaming advertising focus. I think that mattered to the market even more than just the earnings surprises and their magnitude that I've mentioned above. I mean, AppLovin proved to most skeptics that their platform has quite a broad applicability across various verticals as the CEO Adam Foroughi noted that "any business in any vertical can harness the power of our platform." So in other words, it may indeed open up a massive opportunity for further revenue expansion as "over 10 million businesses worldwide that advertise online" can come and be well-served by APP's marketing solutions. At least this is the main thing I grasped from the management's comments.

They finally decided to divest their Apps business to focus on becoming a "pure advertising platform" going forward. Indeed, the Apps revenue is still in stagnation and it has a lower adjusted EBITDA margin (19% vs. 78% margin coming from the advertising revenues). By the way, their consolidated Adjusted EBITDA increased by 78% YoY to ~$848 million with a 62% adjusted EBITDA margin, so the Apps business's divestment will likely lead to a boost in EBITDA margin shortly.

I also liked APP's newly-introduced "EBITDA per employee" metric, which is going to get their "favorite metric going forward" - it shows the management's focus on profitable growth rather than just boosting revenues at all costs. Speaking of the revenue growth quality, I should note here that AppLovin doesn't seem to have the SBC problem of Palantir (PLTR)", meaning that APP isn't overpaying for the top-line boost, and their SBC-to-sales of just 6.84% will likely lead to minimal share count expansion in the future (a good sign for existing shareholders).

Moreover, the firm has repurchased (or withheld) ~25.7 million shares for about $2.1 billion during the full year, and so generating a full-year CFO of over $2 billion and an FCF of ~$1.881 (Seeking Alpha data), their balance sheet's liquidity looks quite solid. I think they'll keep buying back their stock from the market reducing the shares supply.

Looking Forward - AppLovin's Prospects

For Q1 FY2025, AppLovin now guides for its advertising revenue to be between $1.030-1.050 billion and adjusted EBITDA between $805 million and $825 million, so their adjusted EBITDA margin should arrive at 78-79%. So basically FY2025 should become a year of "Advertising-like" margins of 78-79% as the Apps business doesn't bring in any headwinds anymore. That's why, in my understanding, the current consensus expectations for strong EPS growth in FY2025 are more likely to be true than not.

But how close are the current expectations to what APP can really show?

For FY2024 we saw APP's revenue soaring 43.4% YoY, which is much higher than in 2022-2023. Now the market expects them to deliver another strong year, but with just a 23.14% YoY growth rate. Their actual advertising sales for Q1 FY2023 were ~$678.370 million (after adjusting for App's revenue share), so the updated sales guidance for Q1 suggests that the sales would grow by 53.3% YoY (comparing "apples to apples"). According to Markets.us' study, the global AI market size is expected to be worth around ~$214.0 billion by 2033, growing at a CAGR of 26.7% during the next few years. So the opportunity is there, and given that APP is a prominent market player with an already proven platform for advertisers, its growth rates might exceed this CAGR as it becomes a pure-play "AI marketing firm".

So as the Apps divestment process progresses, I believe that the firm is likely to report close to 35-45% in sales growth for the full fiscal 2025 year. Let's assume 40% in the middle.

At just 70% in adjusted EBITDA margin - I intentionally take a lower figure than their 78-79% guided range for Q1 - APP can generate up to $4,615 million in absolute adjusted EBITDA for the full year. So on a forwarding basis, APP stock is currently trading at an adjusted EV/EBITDA of ~33.8x, which is ~16% below today's FWD ratio derived from the current consensus:

Although I truly liked what I saw in AppLovin's Q4 data, even my more bullish-than-census estimates can't justify its valuation.

The real problem that I see is that if APP finally takes 79% in adjusted EBITDA margin in FY2026 and its sales grow from $6.593 billion in FY2025 (my estimate) by 30% (above its end market's CAGR), the implied EV/EBITDA for FY2026 would arrive at ~23x. The stock would stay clearly overvalued as today's EV/EBITDA FWD ratio in the IT sector is ~16.32x.

The Bottom Line - Is APP Stock A Buy Now?

Again, I like the company's growth prospects as the management keeps working on shifting the firm's current revenue structure to a higher-margin one. In the long run, it should bear fruit and lead to prolonged top and bottom-line growth.

On the other hand, even taking into account my bullish assumptions on its above-average CAGR in sales, and taking the higher-end of Q1 EBITDA margin guidance as a base input for my valuation calculations, the stock turns out to be too pricey. So the most rational thing right now would be to wait for a deeper pullback from here: in case APP's stock price corrects by another 20%, I'd be willing to upgrade it to "Buy" as its FY2026 EV/EBITDA would become more-less bearable. For now, the APP stock is still a "Hold" for me.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Republic Bancorp: Upgrading To Hold, Earnings Likely To Grow
data.description:
Republic Bancorp's (NASDAQ:RBCAA) stock price has plunged by around 9% since I last wrote an article on the company. Further, the earnings reported for the fourth quarter missed my expectations. After updating my model for the fourth quarter's results, I'm now estimating an EPS of $5.54 for 2025, which is slightly lower than my previous estimate. I'm expecting loan growth and a lower provision expense to drive earnings this year. The year-end target price is still below the current market price, as was the case when I last wrote about the company. However, the total expected return is now no longer too bad. Therefore, I'm upgrading Republic Bancorp to a hold rating.

Stock Price Rout Since Last Report Calls for Valuation Update

I adopted a sell rating on Republic Bancorp in my last report on the company, which was published on November 7, 2024. As expected, the stock price has fallen by ~9% since the issuance of that report. During this time, Republic Bancorp has also announced its full-year results with an EPS of $5.14, which fell short of my previous estimate.

The provisions expense for loan losses surprisingly shot up during the last quarter, which was the biggest reason why earnings missed my expectations. Republic Bancorp's non-performing loans ("NPL") also rose during the quarter; so, although the company raised its allowances for loan losses, the allowances-to-NPL ratio worsened during the quarter. However, the ratio is still quite high and more than comfortable at 404%.

Another reason why the earnings missed my expectations was that the operating expenses shot up during the quarter, which led to a worse-than-expected efficiency ratio.

Provisioning Will Likely Ease in 2025

Republic Bancorp's provision expense for loan losses has shot up in the last two years. In fact, the provision expense is now higher than it was during the pandemic in 2020.

Part of the provisioning buildup is attributable to the asset quality, which has worsened of late. However, the asset quality still isn't that bad from a historical point of view. The NPL to total loan ratio was much worse in 2019 and 2020.

Looking at the loan book, it's not clear why the management has become so much more prudent over the last couple of years. Republic Bancorp adopted CECL in January 2020, so there has been no change in accounting rules over the last couple of years. Further, the company's loan book is well-diversified, with a branch network spanning metropolitan areas in five states. The loan book is well-diversified by loan segments as well. The biggest loan segment, commercial real estate, makes up 33% of total loans and the second-biggest loan segment, residential real estate, makes up 25% of total loans.

Republic Bancorp has not disclosed the balance of loans collateralized by office properties; so, it's difficult to determine the credit risk facing the company. The company does have some exposure to credit card and automobile loans, which are generally considered to be riskier than other loans, especially real estate loans. However, this exposure is limited as credit card loans made up just 0.3% of total loans, and automobile loans made up just 0.02% of total loans at the end of December.

The management mentioned in 4Q 2024's earnings release that the quarter's heightened provisions were due to loan additions in the traditional banking division (other divisions are warehouse banking and Republic Processing Group). The management did not discuss any problem loans in particular. The allowances-to-NPLs ratio stood at 404% at the end of the last quarter, which is quite high and more than comfortable as long as there are no systemic credit problems.

All in all, I think the loan portfolio's credit quality is fine, and I think the provisioning can ease in 2025, but I'm not very confident as Republic Bancorp has not made the disclosures that I need, especially the exposure to office property loans.

I'm expecting a provision expense of $48 million for 2025, which is 0.9% of total loans, down from 1.0% of loans last year. In my last report, I projected a provision expense of only $24 million for 2025, which seems quite naively optimistic now after the fourth quarter's results. The second and third quarter's results had given me hope that we could return to the pre-2023 level, but I was obviously very wrong.

Net Interest Income Outlook Remains Positive

Republic Bancorp's net interest income finished the year on a positive note. The full year's net interest income grew by an impressive 8% to $312 million, which was close to my previous estimate of $307 million.

I think the net interest income growth might slow down this year, mostly because I think loan growth in 2025 won't be able to match last year's loan growth. The loan book grew by 3.7% in 2024, which is close to the last five-year compounded annual growth rate of 4.2%. The company's loan growth has been quite volatile and extreme from year to year, having fallen by 6.7% in 2021 and risen by 16% in 2023.

As summarized below, Republic Bancorp's branch network is concentrated in Kentucky. Tampa, FL, is also a key operating area for the company.

Kentucky's unemployment rate has shot up since I last covered Republic Bancorp. Meanwhile, Tampa's unemployment rate continues to remain low.

Further, the house price indices for most of Republic Bancorp's markets have gotten steeper in 2024, with the exception of Florida. The growth of housing prices in most states is not a good signal for Republic Bancorp's residential real estate segment, as it affects housing affordability. The dip in housing prices in Florida is also not a positive signal because of the devastation caused by Hurricanes Milton and Helene.

Overall, I'm expecting the loan book to grow by 3.0% in 2025. Compared to my last report on the company, I haven't changed my loan growth estimate for this year, but my average loan balance estimate is higher now because the loan balance at the end of December 2024 was higher than I had expected.

I'm assuming deposits and borrowings will grow in line with loans.

Another factor that will affect the net interest income in 2025 will be the net interest margin. The average margin in 2024 was 6 basis points below the average margin for 2023. After the 100 basis points Fed funds rate cuts in the latter part of 2024, I'm assuming no cuts in 2025. My outlook is mostly based on the tariffs, which have created an environment of uncertainty. The management's rate simulation model showed in last September that 100 basis points rate cuts would have a negligible impact on the net interest income.

As a result, I'm expecting the average margin to remain unchanged this year. The margin will jump up in the first quarter and then normalize for the remainder of the year because of seasonality factors. Republic Bancorp's net interest margin usually spikes in the first quarter of the year.

Overall, I'm expecting the company to report a net interest income of $325 million for 2025, up 4% year-over-year. Compared to my last report on the company, I've slightly raised my net interest income estimate because my loan balance estimate is higher than before following the fourth quarter's above-expected loan growth.

Now Expecting an EPS of $5.54 for 2025

The low loan growth will likely be the biggest earnings catalyst in 2025. Further, I'm expecting the provision expense in 2025 to be lower compared to last year's provision expense, which will support earnings growth. Meanwhile, I'm assuming the non-interest income will be stable and the non-interest expense will move in line with revenues so that the efficiency ratio barely changes.

Overall, I'm estimating earnings per share of $5.54 for 2025, up 8% year-over-year. In my last report, I projected an EPS of $5.62 for this year. I've reduced my earnings estimate mostly because I've raised my provision expense estimate following the fourth quarter's results.

Upgrading to Hold

Republic Bancorp raised its quarterly dividend by 11% to $0.451 for the first quarter of 2025. The new dividend suggests a dividend yield of 2.7%. Moreover, my earnings estimates and the new dividend level suggest a payout ratio of 33%, so the dividend appears safe from any cuts.

I'm using the historical average price-to-earnings ("P/E") and price-to-tangible book ("P/TB") multiples to value Republic Bancorp.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple with my forecast for EPS gives a target price of $60.3 for December 31, 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple with my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $63.7 for December 31, 2025.

The average target price comes out to $62.0. In my last report, I gave a target price of $62.2 for December 2025 and adopted a sell rating. My target price is a bit lower now, as my earnings estimate is also a bit lower.

My new target price suggests an 8.3% downside from the February 19 closing market price. Adding the dividend yield of 2.7% to the price downside gives a total expected return of negative 5.7%. This isn't so bad; therefore, I think it's time to upgrade Republic Bancorp to a hold rating.

As of December 31st, 2024, the Voss Value Master Fund’s total gross exposure stood at 184.3% and the net long exposure was 84.9%. The top 10 longs had a weight of 72.5%, and our top 10 shorts had a weight of -14.6%.

Voss Value Master Fund assets under management stood at approximately $337.2 million and Firm assets stood at approximately $1.2 billion as of December 31st, 2024.

The market is a perpetual paradox machine where one does not go to fulfill the need for clarity and cohesion. Instead, one will find a continuing oscillation between rationalizations for extreme moves. The cheapest quartile of stocks is cheaper now than in 2014 and the most expensive quartile is much more expensive, partly a function of capital flooding out of active and into passive (passive now ~54% of total US equity assets 1). The divergences between the valuation of similar assets can often reach proportions that are hard to fathom and can’t easily be explained by anything empirical, but the best long/short equity opportunities are born from these extreme contradictions that we find ongoing today, thus there is a superb stock picking backdrop.

The consistency of price momentum factor performance has put it in rarified air, such that many prominent parts of The Great Humiliator are breaching the confines of historical probability and trampling on the Gaussian curve. Rolling 9-month price momentum of large cap stocks has only done better on four occasions in market history, with the end of 2020/start of 2021 being one of those rare time periods. 2

Retail investor inflows and flows across nearly all asset classes have shattered records so far in 2025 3, underscoring a persistent imbalance—there is too much capital chasing too few assets combined with limited new asset creation. In this environment, a new breed of nihilistic traders has spawned over 40 million intrinsically worthless cryptocurrencies, all while deriding rigorous fundamental analysis and detailed valuation.

The S&P 500 was up 27% in 2024 with just 8% EPS growth expected for the year—the P/E multiple expanded by 15% even with the 10-year yield up >70 bps. Q4 2024 earnings have been solid so far and shown an acceleration of growth, with every sector beating consensus expectations with total year-over-year growth clocking in at a robust 16.7% (7% above expectations) 4. At a pricey 23x 2025 earnings with 14% EPS growth already embedded in that forward P/E ratio (the trailing 20+ year average EPS growth has been closer to 7.2%), we will see if historically “normal” valuations are given a peaceful reentrance onto Wall Street ushered in by continued boomy EPS growth the next several years or if valuations will violently plop down under the weight of unrealistic earnings growth expectations. In other words, sustained earnings growth and a strong ROIC in the tech sector will be crucial to keeping overall market multiples above 20x, with the market’s perception of burgeoning AI-related capex playing a key role.

Small caps keep trying to hold alpha’s face, but it quickly turns away time and time again. Regardless of how factor or style leadership shakes out this year, our investment team’s main theme and focus coming into 2025 has been on small cap M&A and special situations as we felt there has been pent up demand for deals after two years of dismal deal flow. This emphasis on M&A is paying early dividends with three quick wins from acquisitions in our long book year-to-date: SWI, CTS CN, PLYA.

SolarWinds (SWI) makes for an interesting case study of low expectations, as we had rarely witnessed such apathy across both the buyside and sell side, along with a strange reluctance to move on from the past cybersecurity breach in 2020 despite clear and ongoing operational execution for several years in a row. We wrote in our Q1 2024 letter that “we think the odds are elevated that the company will receive a buyout offer from a different PE fund within the next year or so.” Given the fact pattern, it was a large position for the fund and Voss was the largest active outside shareholder when the buyout was announced. SWI was undergoing a perpetual license to subscription transition with subscription ARR growth of 36% in the most recent quarter, with total ARR growth having accelerated up to 8.4% from low single digits several quarters ago, and total revenue growth improving from 0% to 5%. Where the market saw a sleepy low/no growth legacy software company, we saw a clear deleveraging story with improving net revenue retention, an ongoing acceleration of total ARR growth (driven by an efficient sales force) occurring since 2022, and a 52%+ on the software “Rule of 40” scale trading for under 9x FCF. The frequent subject of buyout rumors the last few years, SWI finally found a new home in Turn/River Capital at 5.2x EV/Revenue and 13.8x our 2025 FCF estimates, a little below our base case valuation, but a win/win for all stakeholders.

Converge Technology Solutions Group (CTS CN)(OTCQX:CTSDF), a technology services company and distributor in the vein of CDW, fit into the same depressed sentiment, low valuation penalty box as SWI. Voss first became interested in the company as they were going through a strategic review a little over a year ago while trading at around 5x FCF. Although that review did not result in a buyout, we began adding to our position as we witnessed the newly appointed CFO (with glowing reviews from his previous stint at Dye & Durham (DYNDF)) unlock an enormous amount of free cash flow (~$275 million on a market cap of ~$700 million) and de-lever the company to well below one times EBITDA, all while maintaining positive revenue growth and beating EBITDA expectations. Although the company ended that streak in Q3 with a miss and guided down, we witnessed similarly challenged numbers across the trading comp set in names like CDW and ePlus and determined that it was far more of a transient macro issue than a company-specific issue. Perhaps there was a bit of global valuation arbitrage at work, but the US names shrugged off the macro headwinds (CDW moved from 13x to 15x NTM EBITDA) while Converge sunk back to the sub-5x EBITDA levels they endured as a far more levered company. Although our thesis was the company would sell off their underperforming, subscale Europe business and aggressively buy back stock, it is our understanding that multiple parties approached Converge and they had three offers all around the takeout price of $5.50 CAD (~56% premium) and settled on H.I.G. Capital.

Playa Hotels & Resorts (PLYA) was one of our “easier” and more straight-forward investments that we wrote about publicly. An owner and operator of all-inclusive resorts in Mexico, Jamaica, and the Dominican Republic, we long thought that PLYA traded below both private market valuations and replacement cost. Given that it was executing well and still not getting credit in public markets, we believed the best course of action was to hang up the for-sale sign. When the company made out-of-cycle changes to their executive change-in-control payouts in September 2024, increasing the payouts to top executives in a sale and doing so three months before their existing employment agreements were set to expire, we significantly upsized the position (buying over 6% of the company), even calling out Hyatt as the most likely acquirer. Hyatt announced they were acquiring PLYA for $13.50 per share in early February. While the ultimate outcome and sale price was slightly disappointing (8% below the bottom of our estimated value range), our incremental purchases had a great IRR, as the stock has risen >75% from the time of the change-in-control amendments just five short months ago.

Good things should keep happening to value stocks like SWI, CTS CN and PLYA, and we expect several additional portfolio companies to be acquired as the year progresses.

Portfolio Position Updates

Long Euronet (EEFT)

EEFT’s stock had been whacked down to an all-time low valuation coming into its Q4 earnings report, presumably on the fears of the new Trump administration’s immigration policies (despite far fewer immigrants being deported under Trump than Obama on an annual basis) affecting Euronet’s money transfer business, Ria, along with general apathy in the payments space. We think these fears have been drastically overblown and it is overall economic growth (and construction employment for the US to Mexico corridor) that is far more important to its business than moderate immigrant deportations (let alone the fact that Ria is an international business and the US to Latin America corridor is a small piece of that business). At current valuation levels of just ~5.4x 2026 EBITDA and 9x 2026 earnings despite remarkably consistent execution and a forecasted reacceleration of growth, we believe EEFT remains undervalued with significant re-rating potential over the duration of 2025.

A part of our Euronet thesis is around ATM take rates. While most “FinTech” companies are constantly concerned about take rate compression, Euronet seems to have taken rate tailwinds in their most known and maligned segment, ATMs (which falls within their Electronic Funds Transfer, or EFT, segment). A little over a year ago the company settled an antitrust lawsuit with Visa and Mastercard, who they believed had colluded with each other to put up roadblocks to increased fees on ATMs. Management commented recently that while that settlement was a first step, it was the countries themselves that needed to formally clear the way in allowing more surcharges on ATMs, something they stated is now happening. We believe a significant bump in charges (which come in at 100% pretax margins) after a decade of compression with rising costs could give the company significantly more confidence in growth from their ATMs and could change the narrative around the ATM business as a secular decliner. The last couple quarters have shown the EFT segment generating > 50% rolling incremental EBITDA margins, something we believe is reflective of early improvements in EEFT’s ATM take rate.

Beyond that, the rest of the business is growing fast enough that ATMs are slowly becoming less relevant. Long a driver of the questionable investment narrative of EEFT being a dying cash-based business, the ATM segment now represents only 19% of revenue (and 35% of EBITDA, down from >60% in 2019), with secular growth businesses like their acquired Merchant Services business (from Piraeus Bank in Greece), REN (real-time payments software), and Dandelion (B2B cross border payments technology) increasing as contributors, and Ria aggressively moving from “brick-and-mortar” to “digital” money remittances. We look forward to increasing disclosures around those businesses’ contributions throughout 2025. To summarize, the company’s most maligned business has substantial, narrative altering tailwinds this year, and their growth businesses seem to be building enough momentum to change the narrative (and valuation multiples) on the company from a “secular decliner” to more of a leading edge fintech company.

We believe management’s recent guidance reflects this growing optimism, even if they are unwilling to spell out some of the potential regulatory benefits. Historically conservative in nature, management guided for accelerating EPS growth in 2025, moving their “10-15% EPS growth” framework to “12-16%”, even after noting that 2025 would be an unusually heavy tax year (high 20s vs ~24% historically). This effectively changed the midpoint of their underlying EPS guide from 12.5% growth to over 16%, which by our math implies at least 14-15% EBITDA growth and 10% revenue growth. Given this profile, we value Euronet with a Base Case EV/EBITDA multiple of 9x, and a conservative 13x 2026 EPS for a company projected to grow EPS between 13-20%. Euronet’s growth re-acceleration across revenue, EBITDA, and operating income, along with strong guidance, signals a return to strong operational momentum such that a “Bull Case” scenario is also becoming increasingly likely. In this case, Euronet gets back closer to its historical multiples (15-20x NTM earnings) with improved disclosures on higher-growth segments and waning risk around their lower growth segments. This could push the stock closer towards the low end of its historical multiple of 15x P/E ($170 PT), with potential upside to 20x NTM earnings if REN and Dandelion gain traction (116% upside).

Long Mercedes-Benz Group (OTCPK:MBGYY)

Mercedes-Benz is a luxury focused global automotive manufacturer with resilient free cash flow generation that is being undervalued by the market. The company has actively positioned itself toward high margin luxury vehicles at the top end of the market, providing a buffer from the price competitive mass-market segment, particularly from aggressive Chinese original equipment manufacturers (OEMs). Of note, Mercedes is the first carmaker to have Level 3 (the manufacturer assumes full legal risk) autonomous vehicles on the road in the US (aside from Waymo) and we think they will continue to be technology leaders in the autonomous vehicle space. While concerns about Mercedes’ slowing growth in China and structural inefficiencies in European production persist, an inflection in MBGYY’s capital return policies is what got us interested in the stock recently. In mid-2024, Mercedes committed to returning 100% of industrial FCF to shareholders, with a 40% payout ratio for dividends (an expected ~8% yield at current levels), with the remaining 60% going to share repurchases (set to buy back ~7% of shares out annually at current levels).

At ~6x Price/2025E earnings (and <2x EV/2025E earnings) along with a ~15% total shareholder return, we find that MBG is a compelling value stock with limited downside risk. If the company can further optimize their balance sheet, there is significant re-rating potential. In a base case scenario valuing the company at 6.8x industrial FCF and giving them credit for excess cash and non-operating investments (equal to ~€42B when including its cash balance, F1 stake at a 25% discount to Forbes’ estimated value, and €10.8B for their Daimler Truck stake), the stock has ~66% upside.

Long Subaru (OTCPK:FUJHY)

Subaru is a consistently profitable Japanese auto OEM with most of its sales in the US, not to mention it has the most entertaining auto memes on social media. The company has been FCF positive every year since 2009. With incessant tariff headlines, most auto OEMs other than Tesla (TSLA) (and perhaps Rivian and Ferrari) are extremely out of favor with many valued at ~1x EBITDA (compare this to negative growth, lower margin TSLA at ~100x). Subaru has been further punished due to being slow to develop electric vehicles. We think this has proven a prudent strategy as EV's are now rapidly bleeding market share globally and OEMs are having to heavily discount already unprofitable vehicles to sell them, meanwhile Subaru is sitting pretty from an inventory perspective. Subaru is 21% owned by Toyota (TM)and is engaged in joint development programs for plug-in hybrid vehicles and electric vehicles with the largest automaker in the world to realize more efficiency with shared platforms and resources. Should Toyota continue to try and clean up its capital structure and balance sheet by eliminating crossholdings, Subaru would make for a great bite-sized acquisition (note Toyota originally paid ~7x EBITDA for its Subaru stake versus current valuation at ~1x). Like Mercedes-Benz Group, Subaru recently initiated a share buyback and more aggressive capital return policy, without which we admit that the shares could be more likely to morph into a value trap without a catalyst. The company has committed to returning at least 40% of earnings each year, and it clearly has the capacity to distribute well above that amount. With a healthy >$10B net cash position and an enterprise value approaching zero despite consistent free cash flow generation, it is safe to say that market’s forward expectations for cash flow are quite low for Subaru, and we are getting the operating business almost for free. At just 6x CY2026E earnings plus the $10b net cash on the balance sheet, we think FUJHY has ~60% upside.

Long Amentum (AMTM)

Amentum (AMTM) is a newly formed, under-covered government services leader trading at an unwarranted 30%+ discount to trading peers due to spin-off dynamics and near-term selling pressure. With a $45 billion backlog (3.2x annual revenue), strong cash generation and a clear deleveraging path, AMTM offers a compelling re-rate opportunity as it executes its post- merger growth strategy.

AMTM was formed via a reverse Morris trust (RMT) merger between Amentum and Jacobs’ (ticker J) CMS division in Q3 2024, creating the second-largest public government services provider by revenue. The company operates across five key markets: Defense (46%), Civil (17%), Environmental (15%), Space (11%), and Intelligence (11%), with growing demand across each segment driven by increasing geopolitical tensions, cybersecurity needs, space exploration, and government modernization initiatives. No single contract accounts for more than 4% of total revenue and AMTM’s scale provides a competitive advantage in bidding for larger contracts. About 65% of total revenues are earned through cost-plus contracts, which protects them in a high inflation environment, which would be our base case as Trump aims to reverse a decades long trend of globalization.

The stock has been whipped around like crazy out of the gate on peer’s results and non-stop DOGE headlines but is ignoring its own strong fundamentals after reporting earnings and is cheap on a relative and absolute basis at <10x FCF. With over $500M of annual FCF generation expected, management is focused on reducing the net leverage ratio from the current 4.0x to <3x by the end of FY26, which should lead to multiple expansion (we believe backlog coverage and leverage can explain most of the EBITDA multiple differences among peers).

Jacobs has signaled its intent to sell its remaining 8% stake in the company by the end of June 2025, likely weighing on shares, but this flips to a clear catalyst as that date approaches. Ditto as the two private equity holders whittle their stakes down. As the company integrates operations and executes on synergies from the Jacobs merger, an acceleration of revenue growth would also go a long way towards a multiple re-rating. At a conservative 10.5x 2026E EBITDA, a multiple that will nudge it closer to peers once they have de-levered a bit between now and the end of 2026, the stock has 80% upside to $34.

Conclusion

In the long run, TGH will prove indifferent to every market dogma. In the short run, however, the dogma du jour—such as price momentum—defines him. We reject the rampant fanaticism that often grips the market and fuels investor overconfidence, instead keeping our own fallibility front of mind. Market downturns, inevitable as they are, can either expose hidden speculative vices or serve as a test of our investment virtues—chief among them, valuation discipline.

The macroeconomic news flow at the start of 2025 has been viscous in its density, making it easy to lose sight of an optimal, bottom-up investment approach. A defining feature of the stock market is that it can be made infinitely simple or endlessly complex, depending on one’s perspective. To cut through the noise, we remain focused on identifying idiosyncratic, value- oriented special situations, building a portfolio designed to continue generating uncorrelated alpha amid uncertainty.

Sincerely,

Voss Capital, LP

Appendix:

1 Source: Bank of America Global Research

2 Source: Empirical Research Partners

3 YTD Flows:

  • Cash/Money Market Fund: record January inflows ($174bn)
  • Treasuries: record January inflow ($17bn)
  • Global stocks: biggest January inflow since 2018 ($93bn)
  • US stocks: record January inflow ($76bn)
  • US large cap stocks: record January inflow ($66bn)
  • Emerging Market stocks: 1st January outflow since 2016 ($3bn)

4 Q4 earnings data source is Morgan Stanley.

Common Terms:

CAGR – Compound Annual Growth Rate

IRR – Internal Rate of Return

CAPEX – Capital Expenditures

LTM – Last Twelve Months

COGS – Cost of Goods Sold

M&A – Mergers and Acquisitions

DCF – Discounted Cash Flow

NTM – Next Twelve Months

EBIT – Earnings Before Interest and Taxes

OPEX – Operating Expenses

EBITDA – Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation & Amortization

P/E – Price to Earnings

EPS – Earnings per Share

P&L – Profit and Loss Statement

EV – Enterprise Value

P&S – Parts and Service

FCF – Free Cash Flow

SG&A – Selling, General and Administrative Expenses

GDP – Gross Domestic Product

YTD – Year to Date

Disclosures and Notices:

Beginning January 1, 2020, all investment activity is conducted by the Voss Value Master Fund, LP (the “Master Fund”), which has two feeder funds, and therefore performance figures from January 1, 2020 onward are calculated based on the Master Fund. All limited partners invest in the Fund through one or more of the following feeder funds: Voss Value Offshore Fund, Ltd. (the “Offshore Fund”) and Voss Value Fund, LP (the “Predecessor Fund”), each a “Feeder Fund”. Performance figures for the Predecessor Fund are contributable to Travis Cocke as sole portfolio manager. Mr. Cocke maintains the same position with the Fund and the Fund will employ a similar strategy as the Predecessor Fund. Actual returns are specific to each investor investing through a Feeder Fund. Each Feeder Fund was established at different times and has varying subsets of investors who may have had different fee structures than those currently being offered. As a result of differing fee structures, differing tax impact on onshore and offshore investors, the timing of subscriptions and redemptions, and other factors, the actual performance experienced by an investor may differ materially from the performance reported above. Portfolio statistics shown are inclusive of the Predecessor Fund and the Offshore Fund. Net results are presented after deduction of all operational expenses (including brokerage commissions), 1% per annum management fee, and 20% performance allocation.

This letter is provided by Voss Capital, LP (“Voss”, “the Firm”, “the Voss Team”, “us”, “we”, and “our team”) for informational purposes only and does not constitute an offer or a solicitation to buy, hold, or sell an interest in the Voss Value Fund, LP (the “Fund”) or any other security. An investment in the Fund is speculative and involves substantial risks. Additional information regarding the Fund, including fees, expenses and risks of investment, is contained in the offering memorandum and related documents, and should be carefully reviewed. This confidential report is only intended for the recipient and may not be redistributed without the prior written consent of Voss. The information contained herein reflects the opinions and projections of Voss as of the date of publication, which are subject to change without notice at any time subsequent to the date of issue. All information provided is for informational purposes only and should not be deemed as investment advice or a recommendation to purchase or sell any specific security. Data included in this letter comes from company filings and presentations, analyst reports and Voss’ estimates. While the information presented herein is believed to be reliable, no representation or warranty is made concerning the accuracy of any data presented. Certain information contained in this letter constitutes “forward-looking statements” which can be identified by the use of forward-looking terminology such as “may,” will,” “should,” “expect,” “attempt,” “anticipate,” “project,” “estimate, or “seek” or the negatives thereof or other variations thereon or comparable terminology. Due to various risks and uncertainties, actual events or results in the actual performance of the Fund may differ materially from those reflected or contemplated in such forward-looking statements. There can be no guarantee that the Fund will achieve its investment objectives and Voss does not represent that any opinion or projection will be realized. The securities contained within the benchmark indices highlighted herein do not necessarily correspond to investments and exposures that will be held by the Fund and are therefore of limited use in predicting future performance of the fund. Indexes are unmanaged and have no fees or expenses. An investment cannot be made directly in an index. The Fund consists of securities which vary significantly from those in the benchmark indexes listed below. Accordingly, comparing results shown to those of such indexes may be of limited use. The S&P 500 Total Return Index is a market cap weighted index of 500 widely held stocks often used as a proxy for the overall U.S. equity market. The Russell 2000 index is an index measuring the performance approximately 2,000 small-cap companies in the Russell 3000 Index. The Russell 2000 serves as a benchmark for small-cap stocks in the United States. The Russell 2000 Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 2000 companies with higher price/book ratios and higher predicted and historical growth rates. The Russell 2000 Value Index measures the performance of the small-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 2000 companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower expected and historical growth values. HFRX Equity Hedge Index consist of Equity Hedge strategies which maintain positions both long and short in primarily equity and equity derivative securities. A wide variety of investment processes can be employed to arrive at an investment decision, including both quantitative and fundamental techniques; strategies can be broadly diversified or narrowly focused on specific sectors and can range broadly in terms of levels of net exposure, leverage employed, holding period, concentrations of market capitalizations and valuation ranges of typical portfolios. The strategy utilized by Voss has a high tolerance for uncertainty. Different types of investments involve varying degrees of risk. Therefore, it should not be assumed that future performance of any specific investment or investment strategy will be profitable. data.title: CoStar Group: I Still Expect Growth To Accelerate Back To High-Teens
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to CoStar Group (NASDAQ:CSGP) in October 2024, with my key thesis being that the growth runway remains very positive for CSGP. So far, CSGP has continued to demonstrate solid execution, particularly in Apartments.com and Homes.com. Despite ongoing investments weighing on near-term profitability, I believe CSGP’s growth outlook remains very promising; growth should eventually accelerate back to high teens.

Earnings results update

In the latest quarter (4Q24), reported two days ago, CSGP grew revenue by 11% y/y to $709 million, beating management’s guided range of $693-703 million. More notably, adj EBITDA came in at $112 million, up 47% sequentially, showing that the CSGP business can benefit from strong operating leverage despite aggressive investments in Homes.com. Adj EPS came in at $0.26, down ~20% y/y largely because of the higher marketing and sales costs in residential (management had previously signaled this, so it shouldn’t be a surprise). Looking ahead, management guided for $2.985-3.015 billion in revenue, adj EBITDA of $375-405 million, and GAAP net income of $212-233 million.

Apartments.com continues to dominate

Apartments.com revenue grew 13.4% y/y in 4Q24, benefitting from high multifamily vacancy rates and management efforts in optimizing pricing. I wouldn’t be too worried about the growth deceleration sequentially as it was impacted by lower salesforce productivity in 2024 when reps were tasked with selling Homes.com memberships. I think growth should accelerate eventually as sales productivity normalizes and the housing market eventually recovers. This is without a doubt one of CSGP's strongest assets. For reference, Apartments.com generated $1.07 billion in revenue in FY24 (almost 40% of total revenue). What stands out to me is the platform’s ability to sustain premium pricing power while continuing to expand its advertiser base. To put things into perspective (data from the earnings call), Apartments.com delivers 4.8x more leases than Rent.com and Zillow combined. This is strong evidence that Apartments.com is winning share and is expanding its competitive moat in terms of top-of-mind share—which can also be seen from the solid unaided brand awareness figure of 66%.

While management expects Apartments.com revenue to grow only by 11-12% in 2025, I think this is a conservative guide that gives it room to outperform. As you can see from the screenshot above, the total addressable market [TAM] is huge, and CSGP has only captured just 12% of this TAM. It may take a while for CSGP’s salesforce to become efficient in penetrating into the small and mid-sized multifamily market, but given CSGP's track record in winning in the >100 unit range, I think it is very possible that it repeats its success in smaller markets.

Homes.com continues scaling rapidly with Long-Term Potential

Another gem within CSGP’s portfolio is Homes.com, which grew rapidly in 4Q24 by 181% y/y, driving total revenue to $28.1 million (run rate of >$100 million). In my view, Homes.com could potentially become CSGP's biggest growth driver in the coming years. The thing that is remarkable is that although Homes.com remains in investment mode—Salesforce continues to be in expansion mode (management plans to increase dedicated residential sales reps from 277 at the end of 2024 to 500 by year-end 2025)— Homes.com is winning tons of market share. We can see this from the fact that Homes.com overtake Realtor.com with 110 million average monthly unique visitors.

The thing about homes.com is that it is still a small portion of CSGP's business, which I think is a reason why the market did not react as positively as I expected it to after seeing the 181% y/y revenue growth. But remember that Homes.com is still in its early monetization phase, and it has shown how potent growth can be from its 181% y/y revenue growth in 4Q24 (also showing that users are willing to pay for such service). The biggest concern is profitability, as CSGP continues to invest heavily in marketing and sales force expansion. The current guide calls for ~$900 million in residential investment spending in FY25, similar to FY24 levels, with no definitive timeline for breakeven. This may be a concern to some investors, but I reckon management is not irrational. They should have aimed to turn this unit into a profitable segment. At some point, Homes.com’s revenue contribution will become large enough to drive operating leverage, and that could be a catalyst for CSGP valuation multiple to rerate higher.

CRE Market Stabilization

Finally, 2025 should be a good year for CSGP in the commercial real estate market. Management noted that demand for office space turned positive in late 2024 for the first time since 2021, which is very encouraging as it signals a potential turnaround in demand. Other metrics that point to the same direction includes: decline in total available space, decrease in sublet vacancy rates, and sales transactions returning to pre-pandemic levels. This is an important inflection point because CSGP's core CoStar Suite is still a major piece of its business, generating >$1 billion in revenue, which has a high margin. As transaction volumes recover, I would imagine demand for CSGP data and analytics products to improve, and that should drive up overall revenue growth (note that the core CoStar Suite only grew ~5% in 4Q24).

Valuation

I remain positive about CSGP's ability to accelerate growth back to high-teens eventually, driven by a normalized housing market down the road, better sales force efficiency, and continuous monetization of homes.com. Hence, in my model, I believe CSGP can deliver ~$4.4 billion in revenue in FY27, and attaching CSGP's average historical forward revenue multiple of 10.6 would give me a share price target of ~$125. The reason for using CSGP historical multiple is because of my expectation that growth will accelerate back to high-teens, which is higher than the past two years (FY22/23) growth rate of low-teens (despite lower growth rates, FY22/23 traded at an average of 10.6x).

Investment Risk

Depending on how much more CSGP may invest into Homes.com, the timing for profitability to inflect upwards may not happen anytime soon. The situation in the commercial real estate market remains uncertain. If transaction volumes remain poor, CoStar Suite and LoopNet growth could be weaker than expected.

Conclusion

My positive view on CSGP remains unchanged (buy rated) as the company continues executing very well. Both Apartments.com and Homes.com continue to perform well, and I expect growth momentum to continue/improve. The commercial real estate market is also showing early signs of recovery, which is very encouraging. Over the medium to long term, I believe the market will appreciate CSGP’s growth strength and revalue its multiple back to at least its historical average.
